---
title: "Project3: Data Science Skills"
author: "Madina Kudanova, Muhammad Khan, Leon Lin, Xiaofei Mei"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Introduction

The project Most Valued Data Science Skills aims to investigate which technical skills 
are most valued in the data science job market. As the demand for data science professionals
has grown significantly over the last decade, it is important to understand which tools, 
programming languages, and analytical skills are most frequently required by employers.

To explore this question, the project will analyze datasets containing job postings for 
data science related roles. Job postings provide insight into employer expectations because 
they typically list the technical skills required for each position. In this project, the frequency 
of skills listed in job postings will be used as a proxy for measuring the demand for specific data science skills.

## Approach

### Team members

-   Madina Kudanova

-   Muhammad Khan

-   Leon Lin

-   Xiaofei Mei				

### Communication

Our team will communicate primarily through WhatsApp group chat and school email to
coordinate meetings, discuss progress, and share quick updates related to the project.

### Code and File Sharing

A shared GitHub repository <https://github.com/MKudanova/Project3_DataScienceSkills/tree/main> will be used to store project code, scripts, and datasets. 

### Project Documentation

A shared Google Docs document will be used to collaboratively write project notes and
draft written sections of the project before final versions are uploaded to the GitHub repository.

### Data Sources

The data will most likely be obtained from Kaggle, a platform that hosts publicly available 
datasets. These datasets often contain job postings collected from major job platforms such
as LinkedIn, Indeed, and Glassdoor. We may also explore other data sources such as BigQuery 
or the U.S. government open data portal if they provide relevant datasets.

### Data Loading and Database Design

After obtaining the dataset, it will be downloaded in CSV format and loaded into a relational database.
The database will follow a normalized structure to organize the data efficiently. Because one job 
posting can require multiple skills and a single skill may appear in multiple job postings, 
the database will use a many-to-many relationship between jobs and skills. The initial design 
might include three tables: Jobs (job_id, job_title, company, location), Skills (skill_id, skill_name),
and Job_Skills (job_id, skill_id). The data will then be accessed from R for cleaning, tidying, and
exploratory analysis. The database structure may be adjusted.
The relational database will be implemented using PostgreSQL, managed through pgAdmin. 
SQL scripts used to create the database schema, tables, and relationships will be included in 
the project’s GitHub repository to ensure reproducibility and transparency of the data loading process.

### ER Diagram	

To document the structure of the database, the project will include an Entity–Relationship (ER) diagram
illustrating the relationships between the tables in the normalized database. It will represent the relationships
between the jobs and skills tables.
The diagram will most likely be created using a visual diagramming tool such as draw.io (diagrams.net) 
or another diagram tool. The ER diagram may be refined after the dataset is examined and the 
final database schema is implemented.

### Exploratory Analysis and Visualization

After the data are cleaned and transformed in R, exploratory data analysis will be conducted to examine 
the frequency of different data science skills across job postings. Visualizations such as bar charts, 
frequency plots, and comparative graphs will be created to illustrate which programming languages, tools, 
and technologies appear most frequently in job listings. These visualizations will help communicate the 
relative demand for different data science skills in an intuitive and interpretable way.
